chunks.text: Tsu-Jae King Liu is Professor Emerita at UC Berkeley with contact information including 501 Cory Hall, tel: 510-642-2689, email: tking@eecs.berkeley.edu, and office hours by appointment. She holds the Roy W. Carlson Distinguished Professorship in Engineering and has served in various leadership roles such as Faculty Director of the UC Berkeley Microfabrication Laboratory and Associate Dean for Research in the College of Engineering.

Tsu-Jae King Liu earned B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. degrees in Electrical Engineering from Stanford University. She worked at Xerox Palo Alto Research Center from 1992 to 1996, focusing on thin-film transistor technologies for flat-panel displays. She joined UC Berkeley in 1996 and has held positions including Senior Director of Engineering at Synopsys from 2004 to 2006 and Chair of the EECS Department from 2014 to 2016.

Professor Liu's awards include the Ross M. Tucker AIME Electronics Materials Award (1992), NSF CAREER Award (1998), DARPA Significant Technical Achievement Award (2000), IEEE Kiyo Tomiyasu Award (2010), and SRC Aristotle Award (2016). She is a Fellow of the IEEE and a member of the U.S. National Academy of Engineering, serving on the Intel Corporation Board of Directors. Her research focuses on advanced materials and devices for energy-efficient electronics.

Her research areas include Physical Electronics (PHY) and Micro/Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS). She is involved with research centers such as the Center for Energy Efficient Electronics Science (E3S), Marvell NanoLab, SpeciaLIzed Computing Ecosystems (SLICE), and Technology Competitiveness and Industrial Policy Center (TCIP).

Selected publications include works on energy-delay optimization of NEM logic relays, characterization of contact resistance in MEM relays, and FinFET-based SRAM design. Notable patents include 'Segmented channel MOS transistor' (U.S. Patent 7,247,887). She has authored over 500 publications and holds over 90 patents.

Selected honors and awards include the Berkeley Citation (2025), IEEE Founders Medal (2024), National Academy of Engineering Member (2017), and SRC Aristotle Award (2016). Other recognitions span from 1992 to 2025, highlighting her contributions to electronics and engineering education.
